coredata.dc:title: Counterbalancing the use of ultrasound contrast agents by a cavitation-regulated system
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Desjouy, C.
Fouqueray, M.
Lo, C.W.
Muleki Seya, P.
Lee, J.L.
Bera, J.C.
Chen, W.S.
Inserra, C.
coredata.dc:description: The stochastic behavior of cavitation can lead to major problems of initiation and maintenance of cavitation during sonication, responsible of poor reproducibility of US-induced bioeffects in the context of sonoporation for instance. To overcome these disadvantages, the injection of ultrasound contrast agents as cavitation nuclei ensures fast initiation and lower acoustic intensities required for cavitation activity. More recently, regulated-cavitation devices based on the real-time modulation of the applied acoustic intensity have shown their potential to maintain a stable cavitation state during an ultrasonic shot, in continuous or pulsed wave conditions. In this paper is investigated the interest, in terms of cavitation activity, of using such regulated-cavitation device or injecting ultrasound contrast agents in the sonicated medium. When using fixed applied acoustic intensity, results showed that introducing ultrasound contrast agents increases reproducibility of cavitation activity (coefficient of variation 62% and 22% without and with UCA, respectively). Moreover, the use of the regulated-cavitation device ensures a given cavitation activity (coefficient of variation less 0.4% in presence of UCAs or not). This highlights the interest of controlling cavitation over time to free cavitation-based application from the use of UCAs. Interestingly, during a one minute sonication, while ultrasound contrast agents progressively disappear, the regulated-cavitation device counterbalance their destruction to sustain a stable inertial cavitation activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acoustic cavitation
Ultrasound contrast agents
Cavitation control

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the experimental apparatus.
Reproducibility measurements: (a) without UCAs in OL, (b) without UCAs in CL, (c) with UCAs in OL, and (d) with UCAs in CL.

Cavitation index <ξ> with respect to the acoustic intensity
I
a
in the sonicated medium with UCAs in OL (crosses) and in CL (squares).

Cavitation index <ξ> with respect to the acoustic intensity
I
a
in the sonicated medium in OL with UCAs (crosses) and in CL without UCAs (squares).

Examples of the evolution of the voltage
u ( t )
supplied to the transducers with respect to the time t in CL with UCAs (black line) and without UCAs (gray line) for (a)
< ξ > = 5
dB
, (b)
< ξ > = 13
dB
, and (c)
< ξ > = 28
dB
. (d) Evolution of the transient time
τ
with respect to the target cavitation index
ξ
f
with UCAs.

head.ce:title: Counterbalancing the use of ultrasound contrast agents by a cavitation-regulated system

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: INSERM, U1032, LabTAU, Universit Claude Bernard Lyon 1, 151 Cours Albert Thomas, 69003 Lyon, France

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation, National Taiwan University Hospital & National Taiwan University College of Medicine, No.7, Zhongshan S. Rd., Taipei 100, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation
National Taiwan University Hospital & National Taiwan University College of Medicine

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Current address: Laboratoire de Mécanique des Fluides et d'Acoustique, UMR CNRS 5509, École Centrale de Lyon, Université de Lyon, 69134 Écully cedex, France.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The stochastic behavior of cavitation can lead to major problems of initiation and maintenance of cavitation during sonication, responsible of poor reproducibility of US-induced bioeffects in the context of sonoporation for instance. To overcome these disadvantages, the injection of ultrasound contrast agents as cavitation nuclei ensures fast initiation and lower acoustic intensities required for cavitation activity. More recently, regulated-cavitation devices based on the real-time modulation of the applied acoustic intensity have shown their potential to maintain a stable cavitation state during an ultrasonic shot, in continuous or pulsed wave conditions. In this paper is investigated the interest, in terms of cavitation activity, of using such regulated-cavitation device or injecting ultrasound contrast agents in the sonicated medium. When using fixed applied acoustic intensity, results showed that introducing ultrasound contrast agents increases reproducibility of cavitation activity (coefficient of variation 62% and 22% without and with UCA, respectively).

Moreover, the use of the regulated-cavitation device ensures a given cavitation activity (coefficient of variation less 0.4% in presence of UCAs or not). This highlights the interest of controlling cavitation over time to free cavitation-based application from the use of UCAs. Interestingly, during a one minute sonication, while ultrasound contrast agents progressively disappear, the regulated-cavitation device counterbalance their destruction to sustain a stable inertial cavitation activity.

ce:section.ce:para: Even if the presence of gas bubbles is usually highly undesirable in vivo (in bloodstream for instance), a growing number of biomedical applications are based on the benefit of using microsized bubbles. For such applications, ultrasound contrast agents (UCAs) can be injected intravenously. UCAs consist in small (diameter<10μm) gas-filled and encapsulated bubbles. Since the first generation of this kind of microbubbles (Echovist, Levovist, or Albunex), adapting the gas content and shell materials led to new generations of UCAs with an improved stability and echogenicity, such as SonoVue (Bracco International Imaging, Milan, Italy) or Definity (Lantheus Medical Imaging, North Billerica (MA), USA). They have been widely used for ultrasound imaging to enhance the acoustic contrast between blood and surrounding tissues [1], and are extensively studied for therapeutic applications such as sonothrombolysis [2] and sonoporation [3,4].

In the context of ultrasound-mediated sonoporation, the first advantage of the presence of UCAs is to provide cavitation nuclei and hence, to reduce the pressure required to produce cavitation bubbles and cavitational effects [5]. Particularly, inertial bubble collapse, which is commonly admitted to be one of the main mechanism underlying cell sonoporation [6], is enhanced. While the collapse of a free bubble is governed by the inertia of the surrounding fluid, the linear and nonlinear acoustic response of UCAs is dominated by the stiffness and viscosity of its shell [7]. The acoustic behavior of UCAs in the nonlinear regime has been recently investigated both theoretically [8–10] and experimentally by acoustic and optical measurements [11–15] to describe their destruction thresholds in terms of fragmentation, cavitation, rupture and collapse. The second advantage of using UCAs is the ability to release them at a target site, providing an excellent delivery vehicle for localized cavitation activity [16].

Even if it is recognized that shelled microbubbles have potential for US-induced cavitation activity and resultant bioeffects, both in vitro [17–19] and in vivo [20], the use of UCAs presents several drawbacks: they are not available worldwide [21] and destructive effects of UCAs have been reported, such as hemorrhaging [22] and lysis [23].

In consequence there is an interest in developing alternative technologies based on in situ induction of cavitation bubbles without using ultrasound contrast agents. Most studies on ultrasound cavitation are performed at a fixed acoustic intensity, making results difficult to analyze due to the stochastic behavior of cavitation [24]. Recent studies deal with the design of cavitation-based monitoring and control, in continuous or pulsed-wave conditions [25,26]. These technologies rely on the real-time modulation of the applied acoustic intensity allowing to maintain a target cavitation level. Such regulated-cavitation devices have shown their efficiency in terms of cavitation initiation [25], accurate control of cavitation over the sonication time [26], as well as resulting bioeffects [6]. Considering these technological developments, the question arises of the real interest of using ultrasound contrast agents for applications such as transfection, particularly in vitro.

Thus this paper aims to investigate the interest of using in vitro a regulated-cavitation device in comparison to the introduction of UCAs in the sonicated medium. This comparison is performed by studying the cavitation activity reproducibility and the acoustic intensity supplied to the medium. The combined use of a regulation device and UCAs is also tested. Finally sonicated UCAs behavior is characterized and discussed.

A schematic diagram of the experimental apparatus is shown in Fig. 1 . Two plane piezoelectric transducers (Ferroperm PZ 26, frequency 426.5kHz, dimensions 18×3mm) face each other on the two opposite sides of a chambered coverglass (Lab-Tek, Thermo Scientific Nunc, NY, USA) of dimensions 20×20mm. The signal supplied to the transducers is synthesized within a FPGA system (Field-Programmable Gate Array, NI PXIe-7965R card, Austin, TX) and is provided by the Digital-to-Analog Converter of the FPGA module (16-bit, 100MHz sampling frequency, NI-5781R module), subsequently amplified by a power amplifier (24V, 4.8A, 50MHz, Kalmus). The transducers generate 426.5kHz sinusoidal pulsed ultrasonic waves with a period of 250ms and a duty cycle of 0.2 (200ms time off). The acoustic intensities in the culture well may vary from 0 to 7W/cm2. A flush-mounted needle hydrophone (Onda HNR-0500) located on another side of the well passively listens the inertial cavitation activity in the sonicated medium. The hydrophone signal is amplified (Müller preamplifier, 16dB) and acquired by the Analog-to-Digital Converter device of the FPGA module (14-bit resolution, 100MHz sampling frequency, NI-5781R module). The inertial cavitation activity is quantified using a cavitation index
ξ ( t )
, calculated by estimating the broadband noise enhancement during the sonication of the medium [26]. The broadband noise is evaluated as the mean arithmetic value of the overall frequency dB magnitude of the hydrophone signal spectra. It is worth noting that, by applying a logarithmic scale before calculating the mean value, the weight of the harmonics, sub-harmonics and ultra-harmonics due to non-inertial cavitation is minimized, and their contribution within the
ξ ( t )
values is negligible. The designed cavitation device is consequently based on the acquisition of the hydrophone signal which is performed in every feedback loop (i.e. every 300μs). Also based on this work, two sonication strategies are considered in this study: the open loop case (OL) for which the electrical voltage supplied to the transducer is fixed, and the closed loop case (CL) for which the electrical voltage supplied to the transducer is modulated in real-time to maintain a constant level of inertial cavitation in the sonicated medium and to reach a target cavitation index
ξ
f
. For both strategies, electrical voltages
u ( t )
supplied to the transducers, cavitation index
ξ ( t )
evolutions, and acoustic spectra are saved during the experiments.

The sonicated medium is composed of 2mL of RPMI 1640 medium, supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated fetal calf serum, 200UI/mL of penicillin and 200μg/mL of streptomycin. All reagents were purchased from Invitrogen (Carlsbad, CA, USA). This medium is stored in a refrigerator for a few days after being made. The initial
O
2
concentration in the medium is 7.88±0.2mg/L. The UCAs used for the experiments are SonoVue (Bracco International Imaging, Milan, Italy), i.e. sulfur hexafluoride microbubbles surrounded by a phospholipid shell with a mean size of 2.5μm [27]. SonoVue are dissolved into 5mL NaCl 0.9% solution, and 10μL of freshly made solution, corresponding to a bubble concentration of 2.5 106 bubbles/mL, are added into each well just before sonication. All experiments are made at the same conditions of atmospheric pressure (1020hPa) and temperature (23°C).

The experimental protocol consists in comparing the cavitation index
ξ ( t )
measured by the hydrophone into the medium with or without UCAs for various target cavitation indexes
ξ
f
or various acoustic fixed intensities
I
a
, with three replicates on a day for each condition, and repeated on two different days. To avoid accumulation of UCAs in the culture well, the cell medium and UCAs are removed between each measurement, and the wells are rinsed five times with ultrapure water. Data are analyzed with the non-parametric Kruskal–Wallis test. For all statistical tests, the significance level (alpha) is set to 0.05 and calculations are performed using Statistica 8.0 software (Stat soft, Inc.).

To evaluate the reproducibility of the measurements, Fig. 2 (a–d) present the evolution of the mean cavitation index <ξ> over the 60s sonication for each of the two days of measurements, and for all combinations of conditions used in this study: with or without UCAs in the cell medium, and with (CL) or without (OL) regulation of the inertial cavitation activity.

Without regulation and no UCAs [Fig. 2(a)], there are significant differences on <ξ> values between the two days of measurements (
p < 0.05
for all the acoustic intensities). Without regulation and with UCAs [Fig. 2(c)], statistical differences are observed for three acoustic intensities:
p = 0.049
for
I
a
= 0.7 , 1.0
, and 5.5W/cm2. Fig. 2(a) shows that, without regulation of the cavitation activity (OL), the standard deviation varies from 3% to 62% (day 1) and from 1% to 28% (day 2) of the <ξ> value when there are no UCAs in the cell medium. When UCAs are present in the sonicated medium [Fig. 2(c)], the standard deviation varies from 1% to 22% and from 2% to 13% of the <ξ> value for the day 1 and 2, respectively. Statistical analysis of the UCAs effect on the <ξ> value indicates that there are significant differences between with and without UCAs on day 1 (p =0.049 for all acoustic intensities except for
I
a
= 0.5
and 5.5W/cm2), but not on day 2 (
p = 0.049
only for
I
a
= 0.3
W /
cm
2
).

When the cavitation activity is regulated (CL) [Fig. 2(b) and (d)], the cavitation index <ξ> evolves in a linear way with respect to the target cavitation index
ξ
f
whatever the presence or not of UCAs in the sonicated medium. Without UCAs [Fig. 2(b)], there are statistical differences on the <ξ> values between the two days of measurement for target cavitation index
ξ
f
of 13, 25, 28 and 31dB (p =0.025, 0.033, 0.036 and 0.046, respectively). When UCAs are present in the sonicated medium [Fig. 2(d)], differences between the two days of measurements only appear for a cavitation index
ξ
f
of 16dB (p =025). The standard deviation does not exceed 0.4% of <ξ> value for
ξ
f
in the range 5–31dB and is of about 3.5% for
ξ
f
= 34
dB
. Nevertheless, the variations over the two days of measurements are less than 0.1dB, corresponding to the measurement uncertainties. The presence of UCAs in the cell medium does not induce significant effects on the <ξ> values compared with no UCAs, except at
ξ
f
of 5dB (
p = 0.02
) but for variations less than 0.1dB.

Fig. 3 presents the evolution of the cavitation index <ξ> with respect to the acoustic intensity
I
a
in the sonicated medium in OL and CL sonication when UCAs are present in the medium. To improve the readability of the results, Fig. 3 presents the mean of the cavitation index values <ξ> over the two days of measurements. The results show that, for a given acoustic intensity
I
a
, the cavitation index <ξ> is almost the same in CL and OL cases. In the CL case, the results of measurements exhibit a high variability of the acoustic intensity
I
a
for the range 1–6W/cm2 between measurements. The acoustic intensity varies from 10% to 35% of their
I
a
mean value to reach the same target cavitation index
ξ
f
. The voltage supplied to the transducer, and as a consequence the acoustic intensity in the sonicated medium, is almost the same between each measurement replicate at low (
I
a
< 1
W /
cm
2
) and high (
I
a
> 6
W /
cm
2
) values of the acoustic intensity (variations of about 2%).

Fig. 4 presents the evolution of the cavitation index <ξ> with respect to the acoustic intensity
I
a
in the sonicated medium in OL sonication with UCAs and in CL sonication without UCAs. In CL, when there are no UCAs in the cell medium, there is a large variability of the acoustic intensity
I
a
between each measurement. The acoustic intensity varies from 10% to 140% of the
I
a
mean value.

Fig. 5 (a–c) present examples of the time evolution of the voltage
u ( t )
supplied by the DAC in CL with (black line) or without (gray line) UCAs for a mean cavitation index <ξ> of 5, 13, and 28dB, respectively. For a target cavitation index
ξ
f
= 5
dB
[Fig. 5(a)], the voltage
u ( t )
remains fairly constant over time when UCAs are present in the cell medium. When there are no UCAs,
u ( t )
is larger than when UCAs are present in the medium, and presents large oscillations. When the target cavitation index increases to
ξ
f
= 13
dB
or
ξ
f
= 28
dB
[Fig. 5(b) and (c), resp.], the voltage
u ( t )
oscillates around a constant mean value of about 0.35V or 0.8V, respectively, when there are no UCAs in the medium. In the case
ξ
f
= 13
dB
, the system needs less electrical energy to reach the same inertial cavitation index when UCAs are presents in the cell medium than when there are no UCAs. The voltage
u ( t )
exhibits a slow increase as a function of time when there are UCAs, from 0.15V at the beginning of the sonication to 0.25V at the end. In the case
ξ
f
= 28
dB
, the system also needs less electrical energy to reach the same
ξ ( t )
value when UCAs are present. The voltage
u ( t )
increases from a value of about 0.5V at the beginning of the sonication to 0.7V at a time t of about 28s from which the electrical voltage
u ( t )
remains fairly constant and nearly equal to the voltage supplied when there are no UCAs. In order to evaluate this time for the whole
ξ
f
range, a characteristic time
τ
is defined as the time needed to reach 90% of the stabilized value of the voltage
u ( t )
at the end of the sonication. Fig. 5(d) shows the evolution of
τ
as a function of the target cavitation index
ξ
f
. This characteristic time
τ
tends to decrease from 40s to 18s as the target cavitation index increases from 10dB to 32dB. It is worth noting that the value of the characteristic time
τ
is irrelevant for the range
ξ
f
∈ [ 0 ; 5 ]
dB
due to the unstable behavior of the voltage
u ( t )
over time in this range.

A large number of biomedical applications uses ultrasound-induced microbubbles. Due to the stochastic behavior of acoustic cavitation, the OL case leads to uncertainties in nucleation conditions which result in poor reproducibility of cavitation phenomena [Fig. 2(a)], delays in cavitation initiation [26] and fluctuation of the cavitation level over the sonication time [25]. Today, the control of the nature and the number of ultrasound-induced cavitation nuclei remain a huge challenge [16,28]. To avoid this lack of control, biomedical applications using cavitation-based techniques usually take advantage of UCAs. The presence of these cavitation nuclei in the sonicated medium ensures fast initiation of cavitation, even at low acoustic intensities, as well as a decent reproducibility by controlling the initial microbubble concentration. However, day-to-day variability could exist in OL [Fig. 2(c)] for low acoustic intensities as the cavitation initiation is a complex nonlinear phenomenon, therefore highly sensitive to the initial conditions like the dissolved gas content, ambient temperature and pressure and initial distribution of cavitation nuclei within the medium. In OL conditions, the use of UCAs tends to decrease this day-to-day effect and thus to increase the reproducibility of the cavitational effects. In ultrasound-based therapies, it is essential to ensure an accurate control of the initiation and to maintain a given cavitation level: initiation delays result in a lack of cavitation activity, and hence, of resulting bioeffects, while fluctuating cavitation levels can lead to dramatic cell lysis when high collapsing activity is attained but unexpected [3]. Indeed the recent developments of systems allowing the regulation of cavitation activity lead to reconsider the necessity of using UCAs in transfection or thrombolysis applications. In CL, the results presented in this paper show that the cavitation index <ξ> presents a low variability between the replicates [Fig. 2(b)]. Moreover, combining the regulation technique and the use of UCAs in the medium tends to increase the reproducibility of the cavitational effects [Fig. 2(d)] in comparison to the use of UCAs without regulation [Fig. 2(c)].

Using UCAs, one of the issues in OL is the difficulty to reach <ξ> values less than 15dB [Fig. 3]. Even for the lowest
I
a
values used in this study (
I
a
= 0.3
W /
cm
2
), the mean cavitation index <ξ> remains large (<ξ>≃15dB) and
ξ ( t )
may present variations which can be harmful for the sonicated medium in transfection applications for instance. To reach low cavitation levels (
< ξ > ∈ [ 0 ; 10 ]
dB
), it is even possible to adjust the UCAs dose, or to use very low increments of the voltage supplied to the transducers. The use of CL sonication frees of this constraint. Indeed, adjusting the target cavitation index
ξ
f
is self-sufficient to reach a fixed cavitation index <ξ>, even for low target cavitation levels
ξ
f
used in particular applications for which stable and inertial cavitation should coexist.

Without UCAs, even if the variability of the cavitation index <ξ> is relatively low, the regulation system tends to sweep a large range of acoustic intensities to reach the target cavitation index
ξ
f
[Fig. 4, CL without UCAs]. The use of UCAs with the regulation system [Fig. 3, CL with UCAs] ensures a minimization of the acoustic intensity range, and as a consequence, a gain in terms of electrical energy supplied to the system, and of the overheating of the sonicated area. According to these problematics, the combined use of UCAs and of a regulation system seems to be of great interest to produce a controlled cavitation activity, and to reduce the acoustic intensity in the sonicated medium.

It is also worth noting that, in CL, once the cavitation activity is initiated (less than 1s after the beginning of the sonication), the electrical voltage supplied to the system remains fairly stable over time without the use of UCAs while
ξ
f
⩾ 10
dB
[Fig. 5(b) and (c)]. When UCAs are present in the medium, the system needs less electrical energy at the beginning of the sonication to initiate the cavitation. Then, to maintain the cavitation activity, the voltage
u ( t )
supplied to the transducer increases with respect to the time. Indeed, when encapsulated microbubbles are subjected to high amplitude ultrasound, it has been shown that the number of fragments after UCA fission is similar to the one obtained after free gas bubble collapse [15]. During sonication, UCAs will progressively collapse and disappear. While the number of UCAs decreases with time, the regulation device needs to adjust the electrical voltage supplied to the transducers to ensure a given cavitation activity. The progressive disappearance of UCAs explains why the electrical voltage supplied to the system (when UCAs are used) tends to reach the one when there are no UCAs in the cell medium [see Fig. 5(b) and (c)]. Finally, at the end of the sonication, the voltage supplied to the transducers nearly corresponds to the one necessary to reach a given inertial cavitation level in a medium containing mainly free bubbles. This behavior can be observed for all the range of target cavitation indexes
ξ
f
, except in the range
ξ
f
∈ [ 0 ; 5 ]
dB
for which the cavitation activity is too low to make the characteristic time
τ
relevant. For intermediate target cavitation indexes (
ξ
f
∈ [ 10 ; 20 ]
dB
, see Fig. 5(d)), the characteristic time
τ
is relatively high (more than 65% of the sonication time), meaning that contrast agents are slowly destructed. For larger target cavitation indexes (
ξ
f
∈ [ 20 ; 32 ]
dB
), the small values of
τ
(less than 30% of the sonication time) indicate that most of the contrast agents initially present in the medium are destructed at the end of the sonication. The characteristic time
τ
decreases with the target cavitation index
ξ
f
: UCAs in the medium are quickly destroyed as the target cavitation index
ξ
f
increases.

Considering future in vivo applications of the regulation technique, several points have to be cleared up. Firstly the influence of the dissolved gas content on the regulation process has to be determined, as the nuclei content and the maintenance of cavitation between successive pulses would be highly impacted by in vivo conditions. Furthermore, it would be interesting to study the effect of different UCA concentrations. In our experiments, the UCA dose has been fixed to 0.5V/V% which has been shown to be a midrange concentration. Indeed, the amount of cavitation activity and the associated bioeffects increase with the UCA concentration in a linear way, and saturate around 2V/V% [29]. Finally, a focused listening of the cavitation activity, coupled to an echographic imaging of cavitation cloud, should be implemented in order to ensure a spatially and level-controlled sonoporation. These developments are currently in progress.

In this paper, the effect of the presence (or not) of UCAs in a sonicated medium combined (or not) with the use of a regulated cavitation system is investigated. The results clearly show that in the OL case, the presence of UCAs in the sonicated medium helps to improve the stability of the cavitation activity. Also, the use of a cavitation regulation system seems essential to obtain perfectly stable and controlled cavitational effects. It is also shown that the addition of UCAs in the sonicated medium in CL tends to improve the regulation process supplying further nuclei in the medium which facilitate the initiation of the cavitation activity for low acoustic intensities. Depending on the specifications of the experiments (for delicate media for instance), it can be of great interest to use a cavitation regulation system combined with UCAs to ensure controlled heavy cavitational effects without generating overly ultrasound intensities in the medium.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the French National Research Agency ANR project “SonInCaRe”, No. 2010-TECS-003-01, and benefits from the Labex CeLyA ANR-10-LABX-0060. We want to thank the National Taiwan University Hospital where preliminary tests on UCAs with the regulated device have been achieved, and also F. Padilla for providing us SonoVue samples used to make the measurements presented in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optical monitoring of ultrasound interaction with blood vessels in transparent sh after injection with contrast agents
Effects of diagnostic ultrasound parameters on molecular uptake and cell viability
Feedback loop process to control acoustic cavitation
Control of inertial acoustic cavitation in pulsed sonication using a real-time feedback loop system
SonoVue, a new ultrasound contrast agent
Applications of acoustics and cavitation to noninvasive therapy and drug delivery
Inertial cavitation dose and hemolysis produced in vitro with or without Optison
